feed.title: NASA
feed.subtitle: Combined NASA feed: the main newsroom, the blogs firehose, NASA Science, the launch schedule, the Image of the Day, and Astronomy Picture of the Day.

entry.title: Cosmic Origins at AAS 248, June 2026
entry.content: Cosmic Origins at the 248th Meeting of the American Astronomical Society, 14-18 June 2026 The post Cosmic Origins at AAS 248, June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: AAS Meeting 248, June 2026
entry.content: Physics of the Cosmos at the 248th Meeting of the American Astronomical Society, 14-18 June 2026 The post AAS Meeting 248, June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Fornax Initiative at AAS 248, June 2026
entry.content: Fornax Initiative at the 248th AAS Meeting. 14-18 June 2026. The post Fornax Initiative at AAS 248, June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: PhysCOS Activities at AAS 248, 14–15 June 2026
entry.content: If you’re attending the American Astronomical Society’s 2026 Summer Meeting (AAS 248) in Pasadena CA, please join us for PhysCOS-organized activities. The post PhysCOS Activities at AAS 248, 14–15 June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: NASA’s X-59 Reaches Speed, Altitude for Future Quiet Supersonic Flights
entry.content: NASA’s X-59 experimental aircraft reached a major milestone Friday, June 12, flying Mach 1.4 (about 924 mph) and an altitude of 55,000 feet, the conditions required for the aircraft to make future flights critical to its mission. The X-59 still has months of performance testing ahead, but after those are complete, NASA’s Quesst mission will fly the aircraft over several U.S. communities to collect data on public perception of the quiet sonic thump it will make at […] The post NASA’s X-59 Reach

entry.title: NASA-Funded Research Follows Bird Flight; Birds Follow Their Noses
entry.content: You might think birds skimming over the ocean wouldn’t seek wind unless it was pushing them in the right direction, but NASA-funded researchers have learned that storm petrels find stiff crosswinds worth the slowdown, in return for the clues and cues the gusts carry. In a paper published by the Royal Society’s Biology Letters May 13, researchers from Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution (WHOI) and the Italian Institute for […] The post NASA-Funded Research Follows Bird Flight; Birds Follow Their

entry.title: Join Us at the 248th AAS Meeting, June 2026
entry.content: Join us at the 248th AAS Meeting in Pasadena, 14 - 18 June, 2026 The post Join Us at the 248th AAS Meeting, June 2026 appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Hazard Detection Lidar System Goes to the Moon
entry.content: Enabling landers to autonomously identify the safest sites to touch down Moon craters and their surrounding areas are high-value destinations for lunar missions since they may contain important resources and valuable science targets. However, these sites are also home to dangers such as steep slopes that are difficult to see in the challenging lighting conditions. […] The post Hazard Detection Lidar System Goes to the Moon appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: Botany and Biology Research Wrap Up Week as Dragon Preps for Earth Return
entry.content: Space agriculture, stem cells, and cargo operations wrapped up the week aboard the International Space Station. The Expedition 74 crew also conducted blood tests and installed a new food processor as a U.S. cargo spacecraft nears the end of its mission. The post Botany and Biology Research Wrap Up Week as Dragon Preps for Earth Return appeared first on NASA Science .

entry.title: NASA to Cover 34th SpaceX Resupply Mission Space Station Departure
entry.content: NASA and its international partners are set to receive scientific research samples and hardware as a SpaceX Dragon spacecraft is scheduled to depart the International Space Station on Tuesday, June 16, for its return to Earth. Watch NASA’s live undocking coverage beginning at 11:45 a.m. EDT on NASA+, Amazon Prime, and the agency’s YouTube channel. […]

entry.title: Black Eye Galaxy
entry.content: This March 20, 2026, image of Messier 64, or the Black Eye Galaxy, is a composite view from NASA’s Hubble Space Telescope and James Webb Space Telescope. It shows Messier 64 captured at near- and mid-infrared wavelengths by Webb, while Hubble’s image shows the galaxy in ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared light. Messier 64 is characterized […]

entry.title: Hubble Sees Swarm of Galaxies
entry.content: Looking somewhat like a swarm of bees returning to their hive, this NASA Hubble Space Telescope image features the galaxy cluster MACS0329-0211.

entry.title: Venus and Jupiter: Conjunction from Avebury
entry.content: To see Venus and Jupiter together this month, you won't need binoculars or even a telescope

entry.title: World Cup Fever in Guadalajara
entry.content: The city’s metro area has pushed westward since it last hosted World Cup matches in 1986, expanding across a landscape shaped by ancient volcanoes.

entry.title: NASA Award Boosts Space Technology Research Capabilities
entry.content: NASA is introducing a new funding opportunity to accelerate academic research and technology development. The Minority University Research and Education Project Space Technology Artemis Research (M‑STAR) application window opened Thursday and will remain open through 11:59 p.m. EDT on Tuesday, Aug. 11. The research funded through this award supports the agency’s priorities for exploring the […]

entry.title: NASA’s Chandra Discovers Possible Supernova Remnant in Galactic Center
entry.content: Using data from NASA’s Chandra X-ray Observatory, astronomers may have found a supernova remnant in an intriguing neighborhood in the middle of our galaxy. A paper describing these new findings published in The Astrophysical Journal. Supernova remnants are the expanding remains of exploded stars and provide elements – like iron, oxygen, and silicon – that […]

entry.title: I Am Artemis: Elkin Norena
entry.content: Listen to this audio excerpt from Elkin Norena, resident management officer, NASA’s Space Launch System Program: NASA’s Elkin Norena has helped the agency launch more than a dozen space shuttle missions – that’s more than a dozen crews to low Earth orbit and more than a dozen historic missions. They were missions that helped build […]

entry.title: NASA Robotic Tech Demo Will Advance Prototype Gamma-Ray Detectors
entry.content: A new type of gamma-ray sensor developed by NASA will take part in a robotic arm demonstration on the agency’s upcoming Fly Foundational Robots mission.

entry.title: Soccer Meets Space Science
entry.content: A soccer ball floats in microgravity in this March 2, 2026, picture from the International Space Station. The space station crew tested soccer balls to study how internal mass affects motion and stability in microgravity. The findings have improved understanding of how embedded technologies, including match-ball sensors, can influence performance during play. Through research aboard […]

entry.title: NASA’s Career Technical Education Day Highlights Technical Careers
entry.content: At NASA, remaining a global leader in exploration and innovation includes having a skilledand dedicated workforce. Technicians play a critical role in advancing the agency’sresearch and missions, applying hands-on expertise across engineering, fabrication,electronics, and countless other technical fields. To help cultivate the next generation of technical talent, NASA’s Office of STEM Engagementhosted Career Technical Education […]

entry.title: The Mermaid Nebula Supernova Remnant
entry.content:
Could the Little Mermaid turn into stardust instead of seafoam?
It would seem so in this beautiful nebula

entry.title: The Eagle Nebula and Friends
entry.content: What looks as if it is going to swallow the great Pillars of Creation? The Eagle Nebula (M16) is not a bird, a plane, or Superman

entry.title: Thor's Helmet
entry.content: Thor not only has his own day (Thursday), but a helmet in the heavens

entry.title: Comet R3 PanSTARRS Through Time
entry.content:
What happens to a comet as it leaves our inner Solar System?
Now, the arrival of a comet into the inner Solar System is typically heralded with great fanfare and high hopes that the comet will become bright and photogenic

entry.title: Jupiter and Venus from Earth
entry.content: It was visible around the world

entry.title: Charon: Moon of Pluto
entry.content: A darkened and mysterious north polar region known to some as Mordor Macula caps this premier view of Charon, Pluto's largest moon

entry.title: The Hydra Cluster of Galaxies
entry.content: Within our own Milky Way galaxy, two bright, spiky stars stand like sentinels in the foreground of this cosmic snapshot

entry.title: A Planetary Nebula with Cosmic Buckyballs
entry.content:
What is happening inside this unusual nebula?
Planetary nebula Tc 1, captured here in exquisite detail by the James Webb Space Telescope, is the celestial site where buckyballs were first identified in 2010

entry.title: Andromeda Through Gas and Dust
entry.content: Over 1000 years ago, Persian astronomer Abd al-Rahman al-Sufi published humanity’s oldest known record of the Andromeda Galaxy in "The Book of Fixed Stars" (Bodleian Library MS

entry.title: The Vela Supernova Remnant
entry.content: The explosion is over, but the consequences continue

entry.title: Saturn at Night
entry.content: Telescopic views of Saturn and its beautiful rings often make it the star of star parties

entry.title: Eagle Nebula Pillars in Infrared from Hubble
entry.content: Newborn stars are forming in the Eagle Nebula

entry.title: Supermoon Versus Micromoon
entry.content:
What is so micro about tonight's blue micromoon?
Just after sunset, a full moon will appear slightly smaller and dimmer than usual

entry.title: Messier 104
entry.content: A gorgeous spiral galaxy, Messier 104 is famous for its nearly edge-on profile featuring a broad ring of obscuring dust lanes

entry.title: NGC 1514: The Crystal Ball Nebula
entry.content:
What do you see in this crystal ball?
The featured image shows NGC 1514, known as the Crystal Ball Nebula, observed by the Gemini North telescope on Maunakea, in Hawai'i

entry.title: PK 164 +31.1: The Headphone Nebula
entry.content: What is a pair of headphones doing in the sky? Today’s image features the Headphone Nebula, also known as PK 164 +31

entry.title: NGC 3660 and Burçin's Galaxy
entry.content: The upper galaxy might be more photogenic, but the lower galaxy is more unusual

entry.title: Thackeray's Globules
entry.content:
What are these strange space globs?
Situated in rich star fields and glowing hydrogen gas, these opaque clouds of interstellar dust and gas are so large they might be able to form stars

entry.title: Messier 2
entry.content: After the Crab Nebula, this giant star cluster is the second entry in 18th century astronomer Charles Messier's famous list of things that are not comets

entry.title: The Nebulous Realm of WR 134
entry.content: This cosmic snapshot covers a field of view over twice as wide as the full Moon within the boundaries of the high-flying constellation Cygnus

entry.title: A Collision of Galaxy Clusters
entry.content: This big beautiful spiral shines in X-ray light

entry.title: The Dark Wolf Nebula
entry.content: A dark wolf lies in gum

entry.title: NGC 2170: The Angel Nebula
entry.content:
Is this a painting or a photograph?
In this celestial abstract art composed with a cosmic brush, dusty nebula NGC 2170, also known as the Angel Nebula, shines just above the image center

entry.title: Unraveling NGC 3169
entry.content: Spiral galaxy NGC 3169 looks to be unraveling like a ball of cosmic yarn

entry.title: NGC 1300: Barred Spiral Galaxy
entry.content: Across the center of this spiral galaxy is a bar

entry.title: Aurora Slathers Up the Sky
entry.content: Like salsa verde on your favorite burrito, a green aurora slathers up the sky in this 2017 June 25 snapshot from the International Space Station

entry.title: R3 PanSTARRS: An Orion Comet
entry.content: Comet R3 PanSTARRS might be best remembered as an Orion comet

entry.title: Messier Catalog at Uniform Scale
entry.content:
What are some of the most interesting astronomical objects you can see in the night sky?
Armed with a good pair of binoculars or a small telescope, if you live in the Northern Hemisphere, you can look for the very popular objects in the Messier Catalog

entry.title: NGC 188: Old Cluster in the New General Catalog
entry.content: The New General Catalog of star clusters and nebulae really isn't so new

entry.title: The Conjunction of Comet R3 PanSTARRS and the Orion Nebula
entry.content: Today’s composite image features something old, something new, something borrowed, and something blue! Comet R3 PanSTARRS, streaking across the right of the image, likely originated from the Oort Cloud, meaning it is an old Solar System relic from billions of years ago

entry.title: Moon Setting Behind Teide Volcano
entry.content: These people are not in danger

entry.title: Comet R3 PanSTARRS and Orion
entry.content: Orion never had a sword like this

entry.title: Messier Craters in Stereo
entry.content: Many bright nebulae and star clusters in planet Earth's sky are associated with the name of astronomer Charles Messier from his famous 18th century catalog

entry.title: Comet R3 PanSTARRS Before Rigel
entry.content:
Which way is Comet R3 PanSTARRS going?
Not towards the star at the top of the image, because that is Rigel, which, being far in the background, is unrelated to the comet

entry.title: The Retrograde Dance of Saturn and Neptune
entry.content: What does it mean for Saturn and Neptune to be in retrograde? Featured is a composite of images taken over 34 nights from May 2025 to February 2026 tracing Saturn (brighter, foreground) and Neptune (dimmer, background)

entry.title: Orion over Mount Teide
entry.content: Orion is rarely seen like this

entry.title: Trifid Pillars and Jets
entry.content: Dust pillars are like interstellar mountains

entry.title: Seeing Titan
entry.content: Shrouded in a thick atmosphere, the surface of Saturn's largest moon, Titan, is really hard to see

entry.title: Markarian's Chain
entry.content: Near the heart of the Virgo Galaxy Cluster, a string of galaxies known as Markarian's Chain stretches across this telescopic field of view
